MATH 464: LECTURE 1
RICE UNIVERSITY, SPRING 2020
1. Categories
In this course we’ll adopt a utilitarian point of view when it comes to category theory: while many
people study it for its own sake, we’ll think of category theory as a clean, organizing framework for
a lot of modern mathematics, that allows us to relate seemingly di↵erent parts of the subject.
Definition 1.1. A category consists of
C

C

Example 1.3. = Groups. The objects of Groups are groups and morphisms between two
C

recover the category k-Vect of vector spaces with k as their field of scalars, whose morphisms are
given by linear transformations. If A = Z, then we recover the category AbGps of abelian groups.
Example 1.6. = Top. The objects of Top are topological spaces, and the morphisms are
C
continuous maps between topological spaces.
Example 1.7. = G-Sets. The objects of G-Sets are sets together with the action of a group
C
G on them, and the morphisms are set maps that respect the group action. In other words, if S
and T are in Ob(G-Sets), then a map f : S T is a map of sets that satisfies f (g s) = g f (s)

for any g G and s S.

MANTRA: the morphisms of a category are just as important as the objects themselves! In fact,
the morphisms of a category can be “turned into objects” in a di↵erent category, as the following
example shows.

2. Initial and terminal objects
Some categories are endowed with particular objects that either admit a map to or from ev-
ery other object in the category. I want to use these objects to introduce an important notion:
uniqueness up to unique isomorphism.
Definition 2.1. An initial object in a category is an object P Ob( ) such that for every other
C 2 C
object A Ob( ) there exists a unique morphism P A (i.e., Mor(P, A) is a singleton set for
2 C !
every A). A terminal object is an object P Ob( ) such that for every other object A Ob( )
2 C 2 C
there exists a unique morphism A P (i.e., Mor(A, P ) is a singleton set for every A).
!
Example 2.2. The category Sets has an initial object: the empty set. It also has terminal objects:
any singleton set is a terminal object. So these objects need not be unique. However, it’s clear that
any two singleton sets are in bijection by a unique map.
Example 2.3. In the category Groups, any group with exactly one element is both an initial and
a terminal object!
Example 2.4. In the category Rings, the integers Z give an initial object. If A is any ring, then
in order for a map � : Z A to be a ring homomorphism, we must map 1 Z to 1 A. The property
! 2
that �(a + b) = �(a) + �(b) then forces your hand for every other integer. Thus � is unique! The
A
zero ring is a terminal object.
Example 2.5. Initial and/or terminal objects need not exist. For example, in the category of all
fields, there are neither initial objects nor terminal objects.
2
